Case Study 01
Live · App Store
HealthTech Conversational AI HIPAA Compliant iOS
AskDoc+ — Designing Trust into AI Health Conversations
How do you make AI feel safe when the stakes are someone's health? I designed a HIPAA-compliant conversational AI from zero — trust patterns, confidence framing, urgency indicators, and AI explainability that keeps humans in control.
12-person user research → 3 competing design directions explored and tested before committing
Confidence framing system — every AI response shows its certainty level
3 rounds of usability testing → co-authored peer-reviewed research, March 2026
View live ↗
HIPAA
Fully compliant
9.1/10
User satisfaction
2026
Published research
Live
App Store
User Research — Understanding Health Anxiety
Interviewed 12 participants across 3 groups: parents with young children, adults with chronic conditions, elderly users. Ran contextual inquiry watching how people actually searched for health information — Google, Reddit, WebMD — and mapped the emotional journey from symptom to decision.
Key insight: People do not want a diagnosis. They want someone to say "I hear you, here is what this probably means, here is when to worry." The AI had to feel like a calm, informed friend — not a medical database.
Affinity map — pain point clusters
n=12 · 3 user groups · contextual inquiry + semi-structured interviews
3 Competing Design Directions
Before committing to any solution, I explored three fundamentally different approaches. Each was prototyped and tested with 4 users each.
Direction A — Symptom checker
❌ Rejected — clinical, cold. No emotional acknowledgement.
Direction B — Urgency triage
❌ Rejected — caused anxiety before understanding. Wrong order.
Direction C — Conversational ✓
✓ Warm, open, trust layered in. Chosen after user testing.
Wireframe Iterations — 14 Conversation States
v1 — No trust signals (rejected)
❌ Users felt dismissed. No trust framing, no safety messaging.
v3 — Confidence + disclaimer ✓ Final
✓ Users felt MORE informed, LESS anxious. Core trust pattern.
Prototype — Key Interaction States
3 rounds usability testing with 8 participants each. Core flows: symptom input, follow-up questions, urgency detection, doctor referral.
State 1 — Consent & onboarding
Trust-first. HIPAA disclosure before any data entered.
State 7 — AI response + confidence
Confidence + disclaimer on every response.
State 14 — Doctor referral
Clear escalation. Never leaves user without next step.
User Journey Map — Health Anxiety to Resolution
Mapped the full emotional arc from 2am symptom to resolution. Every tone and timing decision in the design came from this journey.
Trigger
😰
Notices symptom
2am, scared, Googling, anxiety building fast
Search
😨
Google search
WebMD suggests cancer. Panic increases.
Discovery
🤔
Opens AskDoc+
Sceptical. Let down by apps before.
Onboarding
😌
Reads consent
HIPAA statement builds initial trust.
Conversation
💬
Describes symptom
Feels heard. AI asks follow-ups. Not rushed.
Understanding
😮‍💨
Gets response
Confidence visible. Disclaimer clear. Feels informed.
Resolution
😊
Takes action
Knows whether to rest or see doctor.
Journey insight → design decision: The biggest emotional drop was between "Google search" and "Discovery" — people arrived sceptical. This drove the decision to lead with a warm greeting rather than immediately asking for symptoms.
Outcomes & Impact

Case Study 02
Live · App Store
EdTech Gamification AI Tutor iOS
vCode Quest — Making Code Feel Like Adventure
Kids dropped out of coding courses in week two. Tools were either too abstract or too simplified. I designed a dungeon RPG that teaches real programming — where writing code defeats monsters and the AI tutor never just gives you the answer.
3 UX architectures tested: linear course, open world, dungeon RPG — dungeon won for engagement
3-level AI hint escalation — nudge → clue → solution — keeps learning productive
Spaced repetition loop designed into the curriculum architecture
View live ↗
160+
Dungeon rooms
8
Languages taught
3
Age tiers
Live
App Store
Research — Why Kids Drop Out
Reviewed spaced repetition research, studied Scratch, Code.org, Duolingo. Ran concept testing with 6 kids across two age groups, watching where they disengaged. Interviewed 4 parents about what they actually wanted their kids to learn.
Core tension: Games are frictionless. Learning requires productive friction. The design had to find exactly where challenge becomes engaging rather than frustrating — different for an 8-year-old vs a 16-year-old.
Competitive analysis — engagement vs real code
Gap identified: high engagement AND real code. No product there for kids.
3 Game UX Approaches — Why Dungeon RPG Won
A — Linear course
❌ Felt like homework. Kids didn't care.
B — Open world
❌ Younger users (8–10) disengaged immediately.
C — Dungeon RPG ✓ Chosen
✓ Linear progression, narrative purpose, real code.
Kid User Journey — First Coding Session

😬
Opens app
"This will be boring or too hard."
Hook
👀
Sees dungeon
"This looks like a game. There's a dragon."
Challenge
🤨
Reads prompt
"Write code to unlock the door." Not sure how.
Stuck
😅
Asks wizard
Gets nudge → clue → finally gets it.
Win!
🎉
Code runs!
"My code actually did something!"
Progress
💪
Levels up
XP fills. Next dungeon unlocks. Wants more.
Return
🔁
Next day
Spaced repetition review. Concept retained.

Case Study 03
Brand Design DTC E-commerce UX Research Packaging
ArVeda — Building Trust for Ayurvedic Skincare
The natural skincare trust gap: customers wanted botanical products but had been burned by empty claims before. I designed a brand that earns trust through ingredient science, real before/after photography, and honest UX — not marketing promises.
18-person research study: 87% had bought ineffective "natural" products before
Research citations + before/after photos were #1 purchase intent driver
Recruiter toggle — shoppers see clean product page, recruiters see full UX process

Product Designer · Lehi, Utah
About me

I design for high-stakes human moments.

I am a Product Designer and founder with 3+ years shipping AI-powered products in health and education — two spaces where design decisions have real consequences for real people.

My background is unusual: a Master's in Biomedical Sciences from the University of Helsinki, clinical compliance at Beckton Dickinson, then a deliberate pivot into product design when I realised I wanted to build the tools — not just use them.

I work at the intersection of AI capability and human trust. The best AI design is invisible — it feels helpful, never scary, and always leaves the human in control.

🏥
Health + AI expertise
Biomedical MSc + clinical compliance + HIPAA product design. I understand the science behind every screen I design.
🤖
AI-native designer
Conversational UX, explainability layers, trust patterns, LLM interaction flows — not just interfaces sitting on top of AI.
🚀
Full product ownership
Research → wireframes → high-fidelity → specs → App Store. I ship complete products, not just screens.
♿
Accessible by default
WCAG-grounded from the first wireframe. Accessibility is a design value, not a checklist item at the end.
